pattern: Track how far one encoding's cursor has drifted ahead of the other's in units of original-string length, and resolve that drift character-by-character or number-by-number until both cursors finish exactly in sync.
hints: Walk both strings with two cursors and a signed 'drift' counter meaning how many original characters one side has consumed that the other side hasn't matched yet.

When the drift is zero, either both current characters must be equal letters, or a digit run on either side starts a new drift (a digit run of up to three characters can itself be read at more than one length, so branch over each possible read length).

When the drift is non-zero, the side that is behind must catch up: a literal letter there always matches (it stands for part of a hidden numeric substitution and can be anything), while a digit run there reduces the drift by whatever number it forms.

edge_inputs.description: single letter against a single digit representing the same length
single letter against a digit representing the wrong length
shortest possible pair, no digits at all
a two-digit run can be read as one token or as two separate tokens summing the same total
three consecutive one-digit tokens must be considered as an alternative to a single multi-digit token
a hidden run sits strictly between two literal anchors that don't leave room for it
two different digit groupings of the same characters must agree on a common total
a full single-digit run must match literal characters of any content, not just one letter

stress.description: encode the same random original string two different random ways (and sometimes two different originals) respecting the max-3-consecutive-digit rule
stress.note: kept small (n<=9) so digit tokens stay single-digit and the max-3-consecutive-digits rule can't be violated by adjacent replaced pieces
